nativeRange.habitat: Sandy reef flats, seagrass beds, Halimeda-dominated sand communities, and shallow backreef zones. Anchors into soft sandy or rubble substrate via a large bulbous rhizoidal holdfast. Common in calm, sunlit, shallow tropical water across the Caribbean and western Atlantic.

decorPreferences: deep sandy substrate (at least 10 cm) for bulbous rhizoidal holdfast burial
open display placement with strong overhead lighting
low to moderate flow zone to prevent uprooting
stable positioning away from heavy herbivore grazing pressure

avoidWith: urchins that may dislodge or consume the holdfast
large herbivorous fish such as tangs that may uproot or graze calcareous algae
strong powerhead flow directed at the specimen

imageCandidates.notes: James St. John, 1863x1359. Sharp, vibrant single in-situ specimen on Bahamas sand; classic whorled pinecone thallus with holdfast entering substrate. Best diagnostic and quality pick.
James St. John, 2928x1868. Second crisp single specimen, deep vibrant green, clearly showing nested calcified blade whorls. Different individual/angle for variety.
Jean-Paul Boerekamps, 1920x2048. Colorful macro of the whorled calcified blade structure (diagnostic detail); tight crop so kept as non-recommended extra.

summary: Pinecone Algae (Rhipocephalus phoenix) is a heavily calcified calcareous green macroalga native to the Caribbean, recognisable by its pinecone-shaped cluster of whorled, calcified blades rising from a bulbous sandy-substrate holdfast. It is generally regarded as one of the most challenging calcareous macroalgae to maintain long-term in captivity.
careNotes:
Pinecone Algae (Rhipocephalus phoenix), also sold as Christmas Tree Algae, is a calcareous green macroalga in the family Halimedaceae (older sources sometimes list it under Udoteaceae — the distinction is at family rank only, within the same order, Bryopsidales), closely related to Halimeda, Penicillus, and Udotea. The thallus consists of a series of nested whorls of flattened, calcified blades arranged around a central axis, giving a pinecone- or Christmas-tree appearance. The structure grows upright from sandy substrate, anchored by a large bulbous holdfast (the root-like base that grips the sand) containing the rhizoids. Specimens offered in the hobby trade are typically 2–3 inches (5–7.5 cm) tall at sale; mature plants in the wild are documented to reach 6 inches (15 cm) and 2–3 inches (5–7.5 cm) in diameter.

SHORT-LIVED / CAPTIVE DIFFICULTY WARNING: No confirmed reports of successful long-term captive maintenance of this species have been documented in available marine husbandry references, placing it among the most challenging calcareous green macroalgae alongside Udotea and Acetabularia. Despite some retail listings describing it as easy to keep, the captive record — and the parallel difficulty documented for sibling genera Penicillus and Udotea — supports treating it as an advanced species. Specimen decline within weeks to months should be considered a likely outcome even in well-maintained reef systems.

CALCIUM AND ALKALINITY: As a strongly calcareous alga that deposits aragonite (calcium carbonate) into its blade structure, R. phoenix is generally advised to be kept in water with stable reef-grade calcium (380–450 ppm), magnesium (1,250–1,350 ppm), and alkalinity (8–12 dKH). Instability in these parameters is reported to accelerate thallus decline across related Halimedaceae.

SAND DEPTH: A deep sand bed is considered necessary for rhizoidal holdfast establishment — at least 4 inches (approximately 10 cm) of substrate depth is recommended. Without adequate depth the thallus will not anchor and typically tips over and fails. The sand should be left relatively undisturbed around the holdfast base.

LIGHTING: Moderate to high lighting is generally advised, consistent with shallow tropical reef conditions. Calcareous green macroalgae in this family tend to bleach and decline under low-light conditions.

FLOW: Low to moderate flow is preferred — sufficient for gas exchange but gentle enough not to dislodge the sandy substrate anchor or stress the thallus.

PALATABILITY: The heavily calcified thallus is considered largely unpalatable to most marine fish and invertebrates, giving it reasonable grazing resistance in a reef community context. Urchins and large herbivorous tangs may still uproot or damage specimens.

SIBLING GENERA: See also Mermaid's Tail (Udotea sp., sw-algae-058), Merman's Shaving Brush (Penicillus dumetosus, sw-algae-060), and Pencil Top (Penicillus capitatus, sw-algae-069) — all members of the same order (Bryopsidales) with a shared short-lived captive reputation, sand-substrate anchoring requirement, and calcium/alkalinity sensitivity. Species-specific aquarium husbandry literature for R. phoenix is sparse; care guidance for this entry draws on what is documented for these closely related genera.
breedingNotes: Propagation by fragmentation has been reported in hobby literature alongside sporulation, though success rates in captivity are not well documented given the overall difficulty of maintaining this species long-term. Sexual reproduction via the gametophyte-sporophyte alternation typical of green algae is not a practical captive propagation method. Most aquarium specimens are wild-collected rather than aquaculture-propagated.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby-trade source found for this specialty entry. Product listing confirms scientific name Rhipocephalus phoenix, common name 'Pinecone Macroalgae', heavily calcified, grows up from sandbed, unpalatable to most marine fish and invertebrates. Size sold: 2–3 inch plant. Minimal husbandry detail on page. Product image URL: https://mosaicmacros.com/cdn/shop/files/IMG-3964.jpg

additional.name: marineplantbook.com — Rhipocephalus phoenix
additional.notes: Provided morphology (pinecone/Christmas-tree shaped, 6" tall x 2–3" diameter), substrate requirement (4" deep sand bed), temperature range (76–86°F / 24–30°C), lighting (moderate to high), flow (moderate), propagation methods (fragmentation and sporulation), palatability (not palatable), and habitat (reef flats with sandy substrate, seagrass beds, Halimeda forests). Difficulty listed as 'Easy' — considered an optimistic retail rating inconsistent with reeflex captive-record caveat; advanced rating applied instead.

additional.name: Reeflex — Rhipocephalus phoenix
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy: family Halimedaceae, order Bryopsidales, class Ulvophyceae. Size range 6–12 cm. Depth range 1–45 m. Distribution: Belize, Colombia, Cuba, Florida, Gulf of Mexico, Haiti, Jamaica, Mexico, Panama, Puerto Rico, Bahamas. Critically notes: 'There are no reports available yet that this animal has already been kept in captivity successfully.' Used to justify 'advanced' care level over retail 'Easy' rating.

additional.name: ReefGuide.org — Pinecone Alga (Caribbean)
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed taxonomy (family Halimedaceae, order Bryopsidales), size 6–12 cm, depth 1–45 m, distribution Caribbean, Bahamas, South Florida. Photographic records from Nassau, Exumas (Bahamas), Key West (Florida).

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
